bloody-mary.what: Bloody Mary is an American mirror-divination legend: chanting a name before a mirror is said to produce an apparition. Scholarship ties variants to 19th-century parlor and almanac traditions; Mass / playground oral tradition spread is documented from the mid-20th century onward.

when.earliest_appearance: 19th century — mirror fortune-telling and related ritual games in U.S. folklore sources; playground chain documented from circa 1960s in ethnographic accounts.
when.source: Folklore studies; North American almanac and schoolyard collections

bell-witch.what: The Bell Witch is a Tennessee haunting legend centered on the John Bell family (Adams, Robertson County). The standard published narrative derives largely from Martin V. Ingram's 1894 work, which claims basis in family papers; earlier oral and newspaper layers precede the book.

when.earliest_appearance: 1817 — reported disturbances in Bell household per later compilation; 1894 — first consolidated print narrative (Authenticated History).
when.source: Ingram (1894); prior regional oral tradition (not fully independently verified in primary contemporaneous record for all claims).

resurrection-mary.what: Resurrection Mary is a Chicago vanishing-hitchhiker legend tied to Resurrection Cemetery (Justice, IL). Multiple driver and bystander narratives accumulated from the 1930s onward; folklorist documentation treats it as an oral-tradition complex, not a single verified incident.

when.earliest_appearance: 1930s onward — layered sighting accounts near Archer Avenue / Resurrection Cemetery in published folklore record.
when.source: Chicago-area folklore collections; Crowe and successor researchers

roswell-incident.what: The Roswell incident (1947) refers to debris recovery near Roswell, NM, a U.S. Army Air Forces press release describing a flying disc, and its rapid retraction in favor of balloon-related explanations; the episode became the anchor narrative for later UFO crash folklore.

who.name: Mac Brazel (rancher, debris discovery in public record)
Roswell Army Air Field (press release and retraction chain)

when.earliest_appearance: July 1947 — debris reports; July 8, 1947 — Roswell Daily Record article citing RAAF public information officer
when.source: Contemporaneous regional press; military statements in public record

ancient-astronaut-theory.what: Ancient astronaut or paleocontact theories reinterpret archaeological and textual remains as evidence of extraterrestrial contact; the mass-market form crystallized with Erich von Däniken’s Chariots of the Gods? (1968), distinct from professional archaeology and assyriology.

when.earliest_appearance: 1968 — first edition of Chariots of the Gods? / Auf den Spuren der All-Mächtigen

vlad-dracula.what: Vlad III, voivode of Wallachia (popularly Vlad the Impaler), is attested in 15th-century diplomatic and chronicle sources for military and administrative actions; modern literary Dracula draws loosely on legend and nomenclature but is not a transcript of those records.

when.earliest_appearance: 1431 — birth year in scholarly reconstruction; 1450s–1470s — primary narrative sources for reign
when.source: Ottoman, Hungarian, and Wallachian documentary corpus

strigoi.what: Strigoi (Romanian and wider Balkan revenant lore) names undead or troubled-dead figures in folk belief; the 1720s Austrian Visum et Repertum episode is a documented administrative-medical response to regional panic syndicated in European news.

who.name: Austrian imperial administration (Visum et Repertum, 1722 context)

when.earliest_appearance: 1722 — Visum et Repertum document; concurrent newspaper echoes through Central Europe

osiris.what: Osiris is a major funerary and kingship deity in ancient Egyptian religion; narrative of death, restoration, and filial succession (Horus) is spelled out in Pyramid Texts and later temple and papyrus traditions spanning millennia.

who.name: Old Kingdom royal cult (Unas pyramid complex)

when.earliest_appearance: Approx. 2400–2300 BCE — Pyramid Texts in pyramid of Unas, Saqqara (scholarly dating range)
when.source: Egyptological publication of pyramid spells; archaeological context of Saqqara

mothman.what: Mothman is a Point Pleasant, West Virginia-area legend of a large winged humanoid; local newspaper coverage began November 1966. Association with the 1967 Silver Bridge collapse is part of retrospective narrative layering popularized after Keel and derivatives.

when.earliest_appearance: 12 November 1966 onward — cited sightings near Clendenin and Point Pleasant; December 1967 — Ohio River Silver Bridge collapse (engineering failure, public inquiry record)
when.source: Regional newspapers; NTSB / engineering reports for bridge failure (separate from entity claims)

spring-heeled-jack.what: Spring-heeled Jack is a Victorian urban panic figure: reported assaults and bounding figure accounts in London and environs from 1837 onward, with letters to authorities and extensive press serialization; historicity of any single perpetrator remains contested.

who.name: Office of the Lord Mayor of London (complaint correspondence, contested corpus)

when.earliest_appearance: September 1837 — cluster of attack reports around Barnes and suburban London corridors; 1838 — Times and penny-press amplification
when.source: London newspaper archive; mayoral letter dossiers (mixed authenticity across copies)

kenneth-arnold.what: The Kenneth Arnold sighting (June 1947) is a documented aerial observation near Mount Rainier, Washington; national press syndication helped fix flying saucer as a popular label for disc-shaped UFO reports in the postwar United States.

who.name: Kenneth Arnold (private pilot, complainant in press record)
Associated Press and U.S. daily press (June 1947)

when.earliest_appearance: 24 June 1947 — observation reported; contemporaneous wire and newspaper record
when.source: Associated Press; regional and national U.S. newspapers

roswell-mac-brazel.what: Mac Brazel (William W. Brazel) is the rancher named in public record as having recovered unusual debris near Roswell, New Mexico in July 1947, leading to sheriff and military involvement that produced the brief flying-disc press statement and its withdrawal.

who.name: Chaves County sheriff’s office (handoff in public narrative)
Roswell Army Air Field (press release chain)

when.earliest_appearance: Early July 1947 — debris recovery; July 8, 1947 — Roswell Daily Record article
when.source: Roswell Daily Record — July 8, 1947; contemporaneous press

betty-barney-hill.what: Betty and Barney Hill are the New Hampshire couple whose 1961 road encounter and later hypnotic regression interviews became the template for the modern alien abduction narrative in English-language popular culture, largely through John Fuller’s The Interrupted Journey (1966).

when.earliest_appearance: September 1961 — reported encounter; documented hypnotic sessions from 1964 in published account
when.source: Fuller — The Interrupted Journey (1966); derivative media

peter-plogojowitz.what: Peter Plogojowitz (Petar Blagojević) names a documented 1725 Austrian administrative investigation of alleged vampire activity in Kisiljevo; the file stands among the earliest formal European bureaucratic responses to vampire panic.

who.name: Kisiljevo locale (Serbian village under Austrian administration)

when.earliest_appearance: 1725 — imperial report and administrative action on record
when.source: Austrian Imperial Court / military administrative documentation

mercy-brown.what: Mercy Brown was a young woman who died of consumption in Exeter, Rhode Island; in 1892 townspeople exhumed her corpse, burned her heart, and fed ashes to a surviving relative per local belief that she drained the family — the last widely cited New England vampire exhumation.

when.earliest_appearance: March 1892 — exhumation and heart burning; March 19, 1892 — Providence Journal article

quetzalcoatl.what: Quetzalcoatl is the feathered-serpent deity and priestly-kingship complex central to Mesoamerican religion from Teotihuacan and Classic Maya through Aztec state theology; narrative layers later braid the god with the legendary Toltec ruler Ce Acatl Topiltzin.

who.name: Teotihuacan and Classic Mesoamerican polities (iconographic record)
Aztec triple alliance textual and ritual record
Ce Acatl Topiltzin (Toltec ruler figure, syncretic tradition)

when.earliest_appearance: Circa 900 CE onward — feathered-serpent iconography at Teotihuacan in archaeological dating; Postclassic codices for Aztec layer
when.source: Archaeology; Museo Nacional de Antropología publications; scholarly codex editions

anansi.what: Anansi is the Akan spider-trickster of West African oral literature, attested in published Ashanti collections and re-performed across the Caribbean and Americas after the trans slave trade; tales are pedagogical and comic, not a single authored text.

when.earliest_appearance: Oral corpus predates systematic publication; 1923 — Rattray Ashanti monograph documents spider-trickster cycle for international scholarship
when.source: R.S. Rattray — Ashanti (1923); Caribbean folklore anthologies
